g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about the fundamental ACID properties that ensure reliable database transactions.
A transaction is a logical unit of work that transforms the database from one consistent state to another.
g.text: A logical unit of work consisting of

g.data-narration: ACID stands for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability - four essential properties for database reliability.
Atomicity ensures that a transaction executes as an indivisible unit - either ALL operations succeed or NONE succeed.
Consistency ensures a transaction preserves all database constraints, leaving the database in a valid state after completion.
Isolation ensures concurrent transactions execute as if serialized, preventing interference between simultaneous operations.
Durability guarantees committed transaction results persist permanently, surviving system failures and crashes.
In ATM withdrawal, atomicity ensures the customer either gets cash with balance reduced, or neither happens.
In ticket booking, isolation prevents overselling - only the correct number of seats can be reserved concurrently.
A transaction goes through states: Active, Partially Committed, Committed, and Aborted based on ACID execution.
Four isolation levels trade consistency for performance: Serializable, Repeatable Read, Read Committed, and Read Uncommitted.
Commit makes all changes permanent; Rollback undoes all changes to restore the database to its original state.
Atomicity is all-or-nothing execution, while consistency ensures valid data states - they are distinct but related properties.
g.text: Higher Isolation = More Consistency but Lower Concurrency

g.data-narration: ACID properties ensure reliable database transactions: Atomicity for all-or-nothing, Consistency for valid states, Isolation for concurrency, Durability for permanence.
g.text: Remember: ACID is the foundation for

g.data-narration: Master these ACID properties to excel in your university exams and build robust database applications.
g.text: 🎓 Master ACID properties for database success!
Practice with real-world examples and understand the trade-offs
